title: Multiple Myeloma
oneLiner: Plasma cell neoplasm causing CRAB (hyperCalcaemia, Renal failure, Anaemia, Bone lesions); common in elderly; incurable but treatable.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypercalcaemic crisis** – corrected Ca²⁺ >3.0 mmol/L + confusion/ECG (QTc shortening). *Killer sign: acute confusion + constipation + polyuria.*
- **Acute kidney injury (cast nephropathy)** – rapid creatinine rise, low urine output, bland sediment. *Killer sign: oliguria despite fluid resuscitation + urine negative for blood/protein but positive for Bence Jones.*
- **Spinal cord compression** – back pain + bilateral leg weakness/paraesthesia + urinary retention. *Killer sign: perineal (saddle) anaesthesia or new bowel/bladder dysfunction.*
- **Hyperviscosity syndrome** – bleeding (epistaxis, gingival), blurred vision, headache, altered mental state. *Killer sign: fundoscopy showing “sausage-link” retinal veins.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Back pain (often thoracic/lumbar, night pain), pathological fracture, fatigue, weight loss, recurrent infections (pneumonia, UTI), thirst/polyuria (hypercalcaemia), leg swelling/oedema (renal), epistaxis/purpura (hyperviscosity).
- **Exam findings:** Pallor (anaemia), bony tenderness (spine, ribs, pelvis), hepatomegaly/splenomegaly (uncommon), signs of hypercalcaemia (confusion, muscle weakness), signs of CKD (fluid overload, hypertension), peripheral oedema, retinal vein engorgement (hyperviscosity).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **MGUS** – M-protein <30 g/L, no CRAB, bone marrow plasma cells <10%.
- **Waldenström’s macroglobulinaemia** – IgM monoclonal protein, hyperviscosity, no lytic bone lesions, lymphadenopathy common.
- **POEMS syndrome** – polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M-protein (usually λ), skin changes; no hypercalcaemia or renal failure.
- **Lymphoma with paraprotein** – lymphadenopathy/hepatosplenomegaly prominent, bone lesions atypical, biopsy shows lymphoma not plasma cells.
- **Metastatic carcinoma** (breast, lung, kidney) – lytic bone lesions but no M-protein, no clonal plasma cells in marrow.
- **Splitter:** Marrow plasma cells >10% + any CRAB → myeloma; all other diagnoses have <10% or no CRAB. *Single bifurcating test: bone marrow aspiration with immunophenotyping.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Urinalysis (protein, Bence Jones by heat test if available; send 24h urine), ECG (QTc in hypercalcaemia), fundoscopy (hyperviscosity), neurological exam (sensation, power, anal tone, reflex testing).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U+E, LFT, corrected Ca²⁺, albumin, LDH, β2-microglobulin, ESR, serum immunoglobulins, serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation (identify M-protein isotype), serum free light chains (kappa/lambda ratio).
- **Imaging:** Whole-body low-dose CT (first line per NICE) or skeletal survey (skull, spine, pelvis, femurs, humeri). MRI whole spine if any suspicion of cord compression or back pain with neurology. PET-CT if solitary plasmacytoma or extramedullary disease.
- **Special tests:** Bone marrow aspirate + trephine (clonal plasma cells >10% by immunohistochemistry/flow cytometry), cytogenetics/FISH (del17p, t(4;14), t(14;16), gain 1q, etc.).
- **Diagnostic criteria (IMWG):** Clonal plasma cells >10% OR biopsy-proven plasmacytoma plus ≥1 myeloma-defining event:
    - **CRAB:** Hypercalcaemia (Ca >2.75 mmol/L), Renal failure (Cr >177 μmol/L or eGFR <40), Anaemia (Hb <100 g/L), Bone lesions (≥1 osteolytic lesion on CT/MRI/PET)
    - **SLiM:** ≥60% marrow plasma cells, serum free light chain ratio ≥100 (involved/uninvolved), >1 focal bone lesion on MRI.
- **Never-miss test:** Serum free light chains + urine Bence Jones protein (immunofixation) if serum electrophoresis is negative but clinical suspicion is high. Also: **MRI whole spine** when neurological symptoms present – missing a cord compression is catastrophic.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **Hypercalcaemia:** IV 0.9% saline 3–4 L/24h (monitor fluid balance in elderly); bisphosphonate: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over >15 min (check eGFR first; avoid if eGFR <30 unless specialist advice) or pamidronate 90 mg IV; avoid calcium-containing fluids.
    - **Suspected cord compression:** High-dose dexamethasone 16 mg IV/PO stat, then 8 mg BD (or per local protocol); urgent MRI whole spine; orthopaedic/neurosurgery consult for decompression/stabilisation.
    - **Hyperviscosity syndrome:** Urgent plasma exchange (therapeutic apheresis) until symptoms resolve; avoid transfusion unless life-threatening anaemia (can worsen viscosity).
    - **AKI:** IV 0.9% saline 3 L/24h; stop nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, ACEi, diuretics if not needed); consider forced alkaline diuresis? Only with haematology advice (use sodium bicarbonate). Avoid contrast studies unless essential.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Confirm diagnosis (marrow, FISH). Start induction chemotherapy per local protocol (e.g., VCD: bortezomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone; or DaraVCD: daratumumab + VCD; or VRd: bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone in transplant-eligible patients). **Dose check local policy – do not prescribe empirically.**
    - VTE prophylaxis: Patients on immunomodulatory drugs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide, thalidomide) plus dexamethasone have high VTE risk – give LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, or DOAC if no renal impairment) per local policy.
    - Bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid or pamidronate) continued monthly for bone disease; prescribe oral calcium + vitamin D (500–1000 mg elemental calcium daily + cholecalciferol 800–1000 IU daily) to prevent hypocalcaemia.
    - Monitor for tumour lysis syndrome (especially if high tumour burden): check urate, K+, PO4, Ca²⁺; give allopurinol or rasburicase if needed.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Maintenance therapy (lenalidomide or bortezomib) ± autologous stem cell transplant (if eligible). Bisphosphonate continued up to 2 years then reassess (risk of osteonecrosis of jaw – ensure dental check before starting).
    - Vaccinate: pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPV23), influenza, COVID-19, Hib/MenC. Consider IVIg for patients with severe/prolonged hypogammaglobulinaemia and recurrent infections.
    - Monitor every 2–3 months: serum immunoglobulins, FLC, FBC, U+E, Ca, LDH, β2-microglobulin.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1.5–3 L/24h (depending on calcium, renal function, fluid balance status; reduce in heart failure or fluid overload)
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if high-risk with immunomodulators) or tinzaparin per local protocol; avoid in severe bleeding risk
    - **Diet:** normal, ensure adequate hydration; avoid NSAIDs; avoid raw shellfish (high phosphate) if renal failure; monitor appetite/weight
    - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2; if stable, 4-hourly; if on high-dose steroids OR critically ill, 1–2 hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (consult local protocol; do not prescribe blindly):**
    - **Dexamethasone:** 40 mg PO/IV on days 1,8,15,22 (for VCD/VRd) or 16 mg BD for cord compression (then taper). *Risk of hyperglycaemia, psychosis, GI bleed.*
    - **Bortezomib:** 1.3 mg/m² SC (preferred, less neuropathy) on days 1,8,15,22 of 28-day cycle. *Check for pre-existing peripheral neuropathy; adjust dose if grade ≥2.*
    - **Lenalidomide:** 25 mg PO daily days 1–21 of 28-day cycle (adjusted for eGFR: eGFR 30–50 → 10 mg; eGFR <30 → 5 mg every 48h or consider alternative). *Teratogenic; require iPLEDGE-like pregnancy prevention programme (UK: Pregnancy Prevention Programme).*
    - **Cyclophosphamide:** 500 mg/m² PO/IV on days 1 and 8 (in VCD) – dose reduce in renal failure.
    - **Daratumumab:** 16 mg/kg IV (first dose split over 2 days) – monitor for infusion reactions; premedicate with anthistamines, corticosteroids, antipyretics.
- **Common alternatives:** Thalidomide 50–200 mg PO daily + dexamethasone (neuropathy, sedation); Pomalidomide (after lenalidomide failure); Carfilzomib (IV, cardiac risk), Ixazomib (oral, for maintenance).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
    - Lenalidomide + strong CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors – adjust dose? [check local policy].
    - Bortezomib: avoid concurrent strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin) – may reduce efficacy.
    - Dexamethasone: interacts with anticoagulants (warfarin, DOAC) – increased bleeding risk; monitor INR.
    - Bisphosphonates: avoid in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) – risk of acute tubular necrosis; dose adjust zoledronic acid: if eGFR 30–60, infuse over >15 min at reduced rate? [check product SPC].
    - Lenalidomide + LMWH/DOAC: increased bleeding risk; monitor platelet count (avoid if platelets <30 ×10⁹/L).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Bortezomib: no dose adjustment for renal impairment, but avoid in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C) without specialist advice.
    - Lenalidomide: **mandatory dose reduction** for renal impairment (see above); major risk of neutropenia/thrombocytopenia.
    - Cyclophosphamide: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (50% reduction); consider alternative if dialysis.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid) – giving without checking renal function or without calcium/vitamin D supplements can cause severe hypocalcaemia and acute renal failure.** *If eGFR <30, use pamidronate with dose reduction [check local policy] or delay until renal function improves.*
    - **Lenalidomide – prescribing standard 25 mg dose in CKD; must adjust for eGFR, otherwise risk of severe myelotoxicity.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Hypercalcaemia, acute kidney injury (cast nephropathy), spinal cord compression, pathological fracture (especially femur), hyperviscosity syndrome, tumour lysis syndrome (rare but high risk in high tumour burden), infection (pneumonia, sepsis, UTI), neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, VTE (especially with immunomodulators), steroid-induced hyperglycaemia/psychosis/osteoporosis.
- **Chronic:** Osteoporosis/osteopenia from myeloma and steroids, peripheral neuropathy (bortezomib, thalidomide – cumulative), renal impairment (long-term, drug-induced or progressive), hypogammaglobulinaemia (recurrent infections), avascular necrosis of femoral head (steroids), chemotherapy-related myelodysplasia/secondary leukaemia.
sections.Prognosis: - Median survival 5–10 years for standard-risk (ISS stage I, normal cytogenetics) to <3 years for high-risk (ISS stage III, del17p, t(4;14), t(14;16)). Incurable but responsive to sequential therapy. Autologous stem cell transplant can prolong remission in eligible patients.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Send serum free light chains** in any elderly patient with unexplained back pain, renal failure, or anaemia – even if serum electrophoresis is normal.
2. **Start bisphosphonates ASAP** for hypercalcaemia or lytic bone lesions, but always check eGFR and give calcium/vitamin D supplements to prevent hypocalcaemia.
3. **Treat spinal cord compression “FAT”** – For analgesia, dexamethasone, and urgent MRI. Do not wait for orthopaedic consult before starting steroids.

rawMarkdown:
## Multiple Myeloma
**One-liner:** Plasma cell neoplasm causing CRAB (hyperCalcaemia, Renal failure, Anaemia, Bone lesions); common in elderly; incurable but treatable.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypercalcaemic crisis** – corrected Ca²⁺ >3.0 mmol/L + confusion/ECG (QTc shortening). *Killer sign: acute confusion + constipation + polyuria.*
- **Acute kidney injury (cast nephropathy)** – rapid creatinine rise, low urine output, bland sediment. *Killer sign: oliguria despite fluid resuscitation + urine negative for blood/protein but positive for Bence Jones.*
- **Spinal cord compression** – back pain + bilateral leg weakness/paraesthesia + urinary retention. *Killer sign: perineal (saddle) anaesthesia or new bowel/bladder dysfunction.*
- **Hyperviscosity syndrome** – bleeding (epistaxis, gingival), blurred vision, headache, altered mental state. *Killer sign: fundoscopy showing “sausage-link” retinal veins.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Back pain (often thoracic/lumbar, night pain), pathological fracture, fatigue, weight loss, recurrent infections (pneumonia, UTI), thirst/polyuria (hypercalcaemia), leg swelling/oedema (renal), epistaxis/purpura (hyperviscosity).
- **Exam findings:** Pallor (anaemia), bony tenderness (spine, ribs, pelvis), hepatomegaly/splenomegaly (uncommon), signs of hypercalcaemia (confusion, muscle weakness), signs of CKD (fluid overload, hypertension), peripheral oedema, retinal vein engorgement (hyperviscosity).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **MGUS** – M-protein <30 g/L, no CRAB, bone marrow plasma cells <10%.
- **Waldenström’s macroglobulinaemia** – IgM monoclonal protein, hyperviscosity, no lytic bone lesions, lymphadenopathy common.
- **POEMS syndrome** – polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M-protein (usually λ), skin changes; no hypercalcaemia or renal failure.
- **Lymphoma with paraprotein** – lymphadenopathy/hepatosplenomegaly prominent, bone lesions atypical, biopsy shows lymphoma not plasma cells.
- **Metastatic carcinoma** (breast, lung, kidney) – lytic bone lesions but no M-protein, no clonal plasma cells in marrow.
- **Splitter:** Marrow plasma cells >10% + any CRAB → myeloma; all other diagnoses have <10% or no CRAB. *Single bifurcating test: bone marrow aspiration with immunophenotyping.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Urinalysis (protein, Bence Jones by heat test if available; send 24h urine), ECG (QTc in hypercalcaemia), fundoscopy (hyperviscosity), neurological exam (sensation, power, anal tone, reflex testing).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U+E, LFT, corrected Ca²⁺, albumin, LDH, β2-microglobulin, ESR, serum immunoglobulins, serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation (identify M-protein isotype), serum free light chains (kappa/lambda ratio).
- **Imaging:** Whole-body low-dose CT (first line per NICE) or skeletal survey (skull, spine, pelvis, femurs, humeri). MRI whole spine if any suspicion of cord compression or back pain with neurology. PET-CT if solitary plasmacytoma or extramedullary disease.
- **Special tests:** Bone marrow aspirate + trephine (clonal plasma cells >10% by immunohistochemistry/flow cytometry), cytogenetics/FISH (del17p, t(4;14), t(14;16), gain 1q, etc.).
- **Diagnostic criteria (IMWG):** Clonal plasma cells >10% OR biopsy-proven plasmacytoma plus ≥1 myeloma-defining event:
    - **CRAB:** Hypercalcaemia (Ca >2.75 mmol/L), Renal failure (Cr >177 μmol/L or eGFR <40), Anaemia (Hb <100 g/L), Bone lesions (≥1 osteolytic lesion on CT/MRI/PET)
    - **SLiM:** ≥60% marrow plasma cells, serum free light chain ratio ≥100 (involved/uninvolved), >1 focal bone lesion on MRI.
- **Never-miss test:** Serum free light chains + urine Bence Jones protein (immunofixation) if serum electrophoresis is negative but clinical suspicion is high. Also: **MRI whole spine** when neurological symptoms present – missing a cord compression is catastrophic.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **Hypercalcaemia:** IV 0.9% saline 3–4 L/24h (monitor fluid balance in elderly); bisphosphonate: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over >15 min (check eGFR first; avoid if eGFR <30 unless specialist advice) or pamidronate 90 mg IV; avoid calcium-containing fluids.
    - **Suspected cord compression:** High-dose dexamethasone 16 mg IV/PO stat, then 8 mg BD (or per local protocol); urgent MRI whole spine; orthopaedic/neurosurgery consult for decompression/stabilisation.
    - **Hyperviscosity syndrome:** Urgent plasma exchange (therapeutic apheresis) until symptoms resolve; avoid transfusion unless life-threatening anaemia (can worsen viscosity).
    - **AKI:** IV 0.9% saline 3 L/24h; stop nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, ACEi, diuretics if not needed); consider forced alkaline diuresis? Only with haematology advice (use sodium bicarbonate). Avoid contrast studies unless essential.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Confirm diagnosis (marrow, FISH). Start induction chemotherapy per local protocol (e.g., VCD: bortezomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone; or DaraVCD: daratumumab + VCD; or VRd: bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone in transplant-eligible patients). **Dose check local policy – do not prescribe empirically.**
    - VTE prophylaxis: Patients on immunomodulatory drugs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide, thalidomide) plus dexamethasone have high VTE risk – give LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, or DOAC if no renal impairment) per local policy.
    - Bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid or pamidronate) continued monthly for bone disease; prescribe oral calcium + vitamin D (500–1000 mg elemental calcium daily + cholecalciferol 800–1000 IU daily) to prevent hypocalcaemia.
    - Monitor for tumour lysis syndrome (especially if high tumour burden): check urate, K+, PO4, Ca²⁺; give allopurinol or rasburicase if needed.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Maintenance therapy (lenalidomide or bortezomib) ± autologous stem cell transplant (if eligible). Bisphosphonate continued up to 2 years then reassess (risk of osteonecrosis of jaw – ensure dental check before starting).
    - Vaccinate: pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPV23), influenza, COVID-19, Hib/MenC. Consider IVIg for patients with severe/prolonged hypogammaglobulinaemia and recurrent infections.
    - Monitor every 2–3 months: serum immunoglobulins, FLC, FBC, U+E, Ca, LDH, β2-microglobulin.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1.5–3 L/24h (depending on calcium, renal function, fluid balance status; reduce in heart failure or fluid overload)
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if high-risk with immunomodulators) or tinzaparin per local protocol; avoid in severe bleeding risk
    - **Diet:** normal, ensure adequate hydration; avoid NSAIDs; avoid raw shellfish (high phosphate) if renal failure; monitor appetite/weight
    - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2; if stable, 4-hourly; if on high-dose steroids OR critically ill, 1–2 hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (consult local protocol; do not prescribe blindly):**
    - **Dexamethasone:** 40 mg PO/IV on days 1,8,15,22 (for VCD/VRd) or 16 mg BD for cord compression (then taper). *Risk of hyperglycaemia, psychosis, GI bleed.*
    - **Bortezomib:** 1.3 mg/m² SC (preferred, less neuropathy) on days 1,8,15,22 of 28-day cycle. *Check for pre-existing peripheral neuropathy; adjust dose if grade ≥2.*
    - **Lenalidomide:** 25 mg PO daily days 1–21 of 28-day cycle (adjusted for eGFR: eGFR 30–50 → 10 mg; eGFR <30 → 5 mg every 48h or consider alternative). *Teratogenic; require iPLEDGE-like pregnancy prevention programme (UK: Pregnancy Prevention Programme).*
    - **Cyclophosphamide:** 500 mg/m² PO/IV on days 1 and 8 (in VCD) – dose reduce in renal failure.
    - **Daratumumab:** 16 mg/kg IV (first dose split over 2 days) – monitor for infusion reactions; premedicate with anthistamines, corticosteroids, antipyretics.
- **Common alternatives:** Thalidomide 50–200 mg PO daily + dexamethasone (neuropathy, sedation); Pomalidomide (after lenalidomide failure); Carfilzomib (IV, cardiac risk), Ixazomib (oral, for maintenance).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
    - Lenalidomide + strong CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors – adjust dose? [check local policy].
    - Bortezomib: avoid concurrent strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin) – may reduce efficacy.
    - Dexamethasone: interacts with anticoagulants (warfarin, DOAC) – increased bleeding risk; monitor INR.
    - Bisphosphonates: avoid in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) – risk of acute tubular necrosis; dose adjust zoledronic acid: if eGFR 30–60, infuse over >15 min at reduced rate? [check product SPC].
    - Lenalidomide + LMWH/DOAC: increased bleeding risk; monitor platelet count (avoid if platelets <30 ×10⁹/L).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Bortezomib: no dose adjustment for renal impairment, but avoid in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C) without specialist advice.
    - Lenalidomide: **mandatory dose reduction** for renal impairment (see above); major risk of neutropenia/thrombocytopenia.
    - Cyclophosphamide: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (50% reduction); consider alternative if dialysis.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid) – giving without checking renal function or without calcium/vitamin D supplements can cause severe hypocalcaemia and acute renal failure.** *If eGFR <30, use pamidronate with dose reduction [check local policy] or delay until renal function improves.*
    - **Lenalidomide – prescribing standard 25 mg dose in CKD; must adjust for eGFR, otherwise risk of severe myelotoxicity.**

### Complications
- **Acute:** Hypercalcaemia, acute kidney injury (cast nephropathy), spinal cord compression, pathological fracture (especially femur), hyperviscosity syndrome, tumour lysis syndrome (rare but high risk in high tumour burden), infection (pneumonia, sepsis, UTI), neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, VTE (especially with immunomodulators), steroid-induced hyperglycaemia/psychosis/osteoporosis.
- **Chronic:** Osteoporosis/osteopenia from myeloma and steroids, peripheral neuropathy (bortezomib, thalidomide – cumulative), renal impairment (long-term, drug-induced or progressive), hypogammaglobulinaemia (recurrent infections), avascular necrosis of femoral head (steroids), chemotherapy-related myelodysplasia/secondary leukaemia.

### Prognosis
- Median survival 5–10 years for standard-risk (ISS stage I, normal cytogenetics) to <3 years for high-risk (ISS stage III, del17p, t(4;14), t(14;16)). Incurable but responsive to sequential therapy. Autologous stem cell transplant can prolong remission in eligible patients.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Send serum free light chains** in any elderly patient with unexplained back pain, renal failure, or anaemia – even if serum electrophoresis is normal.
2. **Start bisphosphonates ASAP** for hypercalcaemia or lytic bone lesions, but always check eGFR and give calcium/vitamin D supplements to prevent hypocalcaemia.
3. **Treat spinal cord compression “FAT”** – For analgesia, dexamethasone, and urgent MRI. Do not wait for orthopaedic consult before starting steroids.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not use NSAIDs** (ibuprofen,
